content.text: are a class of large language models trained with reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks—mathematics, code, and logic—so that they learn to generate extended internal deliberation before producing a final answer. Unlike standard instruction-following models that respond in a single autoregressive pass, reasoning models allocate compute to a hidden scratchpad (called thinking tokens) where they explore, backtrack, and self-verify intermediate steps. In agentic systems they fill the planner and critic roles: orchestrating tool-call sequences, diagnosing failures, and producing structured plans that cheaper executor models then carry out.

A reasoning model is fundamentally an autoregressive transformer whose training objective has been augmented with an outcome-based reward signal. During supervised pre-training the model learns general language and factual associations; during the reasoning-specific phase it receives rewards only for

on problems that have objectively verifiable solutions. Through thousands of RL steps the model spontaneously develops intermediate reasoning traces—sometimes called chain-of-thought or extended thinking—as an instrumental strategy for maximising rewards. These traces are not hand-engineered; they emerge because producing them reliably leads to better final answers.
At inference time the model generates one or more blocks of thinking tokens (opaque to the end user by default) followed by the visible output. API providers expose a

parameter that lets orchestrators control how many tokens the model may use for deliberation. Distilled reasoning variants transfer these learned traces into smaller models via knowledge distillation, giving lower-latency reasoning at reduced capability.

Common products include OpenAI o1/o3, Anthropic Claude with extended thinking enabled, Google Gemini with thinking mode, and the open-weight DeepSeek-R1 family. All share the same external interface as regular chat completion APIs, making them drop-in substitutes for hard subtasks within an existing agent pipeline.

Standard instruct models answer in a single forward pass. For a conversational query like "summarise this article" that is entirely adequate. But agent tasks routinely demand multi-step reasoning—constraint-satisfying plan generation, diagnosis across interleaved tool results, algorithmic design, and formal proof construction. Standard models applied to these tasks suffer from three failure modes that reasoning models directly address:
A model that cannot revise intermediate conclusions locks in wrong assumptions from the first sentence. Reasoning models backtrack internally before committing to a visible output.
In agentic loops a bad plan produces cascading tool errors. Reasoning models produce structurally sound plans with fewer broken dependencies because they verify preconditions during thinking.
Without a scratchpad, a model cannot notice that its earlier reasoning was inconsistent. Extended thinking allows the model to catch and repair contradictions in the same generation.

Practically, benchmark improvements are decisive. On competition-level mathematics (AIME, MATH-500), code-generation (SWE-bench), and multi-hop question answering, reasoning models outperform same-generation standard models by 15–40 percentage points. For agent systems where a single planning error costs an entire trajectory, this quality gap translates directly to business value.
Extended thinking allows the model to explore multiple solution paths and select the best before output, dramatically reducing first-pass error rates on hard problems.
The model catches flawed parameter construction, missing edge cases, and contradictory constraints during thinking—before emitting a tool call that would require a costly retry.
Reasoning models produce plans with correctly ordered steps and satisfied dependencies. Executor models downstream see well-formed instructions rather than ambiguous or circular directives.
Thinking traces, when surfaced to developers, provide a readable explanation of model decisions—useful for debugging and for regulated domains that require explainability.
A reasoning model reviewing another model's output catches errors that the generating model missed, acting as a high-quality, automated code or plan reviewer.
By spending thinking tokens on disambiguation, the model produces more conservative, correct outputs rather than confidently wrong ones when specifications are underspecified.

Thinking tokens are generated autoregressively before the visible output. A request with 8 000 budget tokens takes 5–30 seconds depending on model size and hardware—unsuitable for latency-sensitive interactive flows.

Thinking tokens are billed at the same per-token rate as output tokens on most APIs. A request that generates 10 000 thinking tokens followed by 500 output tokens costs 20× more per-request than a direct-output call.
Thinking blocks consume the context window. In multi-turn agent conversations where thinking is accumulated across turns, the effective space for tool results shrinks rapidly.
Routing simple formatting, retrieval, or classification tasks to a reasoning model wastes budget and adds latency without improving output quality.
RL rewards are only defined for problems with objectively checkable answers. The model still hallucinates on factual questions where no verifiable reward signal existed during training.
Setting budget_tokens too low suppresses beneficial deliberation; too high wastes tokens on already-solved subproblems. Optimal budgets are task-specific and require empirical calibration.
Training: reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards

The reasoning training phase starts from a base or lightly instruction-tuned checkpoint. A policy gradient algorithm—GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimisation) in DeepSeek-R1, PPO in OpenAI's early models—runs rollouts where the model generates a full chain-of-thought and a final answer. The environment awards reward
when the final answer is verifiably correct,
otherwise. No reward is given for the quality of intermediate thinking, only for the correctness of the conclusion. The policy update is:
is the advantage—estimated from sampled reward—and

is the model policy. Because the only reward signal comes from verifiable tasks (math, code execution, formal proofs), the model cannot be rewarded for sounding plausible on unverifiable facts. This is a structural limitation that reasoning training does not remove.
Inference: thinking budget and token flow
At inference the model generates tokens from two logically distinct regions: the thinking space and the output space. The total token budget is constrained as:

. When the model finishes its internal deliberation it emits a special end-of-thinking token and transitions to visible output generation. APIs typically suppress the thinking region from the user-visible response but expose it to the developer through a separate content block type (
Full RL training of reasoning models requires thousands of GPU-hours and large-scale problem sets. A cheaper alternative is

: sample high-quality thinking traces from a teacher reasoning model, then fine-tune a smaller student model to reproduce those traces via standard supervised learning. The student learns the format and structure of deliberation without running RL. The trade-off is that the student's reasoning ability is bounded by the teacher's ceiling, and domain gaps not covered by the sampled traces are not learned.

The diagram below shows the two phases: the offline training pipeline on the left, and the online agentic inference flow on the right. During training, verifiable rewards drive RL updates that teach the model to use an internal thinking scratchpad. During inference, an orchestrator routes complex tasks to the reasoning model; thinking tokens remain hidden while the visible output (tool calls and structured answer) flows to a cheaper executor model for step-by-step execution.

attrs.alt: Reasoning model architecture: RL training pipeline on the left, agentic planner-executor inference flow on the right

content.text: The planner–executor split is the dominant deployment pattern in production agent systems. The reasoning model handles the expensive planning, replan, and critic steps—typically once per trajectory or once per tool failure—while the executor model handles the high-frequency, cheap execution steps.
The task requires multi-step logical deduction, proof construction, or algorithm design where intermediate errors cascade into final failures.
An agent's planning step repeatedly fails or produces circular tool-call sequences—a reasoning model replanner typically resolves these.
You need the model to produce a structured execution plan that will be handed to a cheap executor; plan quality is worth the latency cost.
The task involves debugging complex code, diagnosing an ambiguous error stack, or reasoning across multiple interleaved tool results.
You need an automated critic that catches errors in another model's output before committing to side effects (file writes, API calls, database mutations).
Latency is under 2 seconds and quality gains from extended thinking are not measurable (simple intent classification, short-form generation, retrieval formatting).
Cost per call is a primary constraint and the task is well-served by a standard model at 10–20× lower price.
The problem is factual recall or open-ended creative generation where verifiable-reward training gives no benefit.
Every single agent hop is routed here regardless of task complexity—this leads to cost explosion with no proportional quality gain.

apply a reasoning model only when a standard model's success rate on the task is demonstrably inadequate. Measure on a held-out sample before routing all production traffic to the more expensive tier.

Integrating a reasoning model into an agent pipeline involves three configuration decisions: which tasks get routed to the reasoning tier, what thinking budget to allocate, and how to handle thinking blocks downstream.
(or your provider's equivalent) and set your API key.
value. Start conservatively at 2 000–4 000 tokens and increase if quality is insufficient.
in the response. The response contains one or more blocks; blocks with
hold the scratchpad and blocks with
before forwarding the response to the user. Thinking traces are developer-facing; exposing raw deliberation to end users typically violates provider policies and degrades UX.
after the model's output. Even a reasoning model produces wrong tool calls. Run the output through a deterministic check (schema validation, unit test execution) before acting on it.
, and any thinking-specific counters to detect budget overruns and cost spikes.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def call_with_extended_thinking(user_message: str, budget: int = 4000) -> dict:
    """
    Call Claude with extended thinking enabled.
    Returns a dict with 'thinking' and 'answer' strings.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=budget + 2000,   # must exceed budget_tokens
        thinking={
            "type": "enabled",
            "budget_tokens": budget,
        },
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )

    thinking_text = ""
    answer_text = ""
    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "thinking":
            thinking_text = block.thinking          # developer only
        elif block.type == "text":
            answer_text = block.text

    return {
        "thinking": thinking_text,
        "answer": answer_text,
        "thinking_tokens_approx": len(thinking_text.split()),
        "stop_reason": response.stop_reason,
    }


# Example: algorithmic design question
result = call_with_extended_thinking(
    "Design a rate-limiting algorithm for 100k requests/second with burst "
    "tolerance up to 150k/s for 5 seconds. Show the algorithm, data structure "
    "choices, and time/space complexity analysis.",
    budget=6000,
)
print(f"Thinking used ~{result['thinking_tokens_approx']} words")
print(result["answer"])
This example routes a hard debugging task to a reasoning model and a simple formatting task to a cheap executor model, demonstrating selective routing in a single script.

import anthropic
import re

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

COMPLEX_KEYWORDS = {
    "debug", "design", "architect", "optimize", "analyze",
    "reason", "compare", "refactor", "diagnose",
}

def is_complex(task: str) -> bool:
    words = set(task.lower().split())
    return bool(words & COMPLEX_KEYWORDS) or len(task) > 300

def think_and_plan(task: str) -> str:
    """Use extended thinking for complex planning."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=10000,
        thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 7000},
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    for block in resp.content:
        if block.type == "text":
            return block.text
    return ""

def execute_fast(task: str) -> str:
    """Use a cheaper model for simple execution steps."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# Buggy code to diagnose
buggy_code = """
def merge_sorted_lists(a, b):
    result = []
    i, j = 0, 0
    while i < len(a) and j < len(b):
        if a[i] < b[j]:
            result.append(a[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            result.append(b[j])
            j += 1
    return result   # <-- bug: tail elements never appended
"""

task = f"Debug this Python merge function and explain the bug:\n{buggy_code}"

if is_complex(task):
    diagnosis = think_and_plan(task)
    # Cheap model formats the output as a pull-request comment
    formatted = execute_fast(
        f"Format this diagnosis as a concise PR review comment:\n{diagnosis}"
    )
    print("=== PR Comment (formatted by executor) ===")
    print(formatted)
else:
    print(execute_fast(task))
Example 2: agentic planner–executor loop with replanning
This more realistic example shows a reasoning model generating a structured plan, an executor running each step, and the reasoning model replanning when a step fails.

Consider a production code-review agent for a team shipping 50 pull requests per day. A naive implementation routes every PR diff to a reasoning model, costing approximately $0.50–2.00 per review and introducing 15–30 second wait times. A two-tier architecture reduces cost by 70–85% without measurable quality loss on routine reviews.

The routing heuristic classifies each PR as requiring deep analysis (changed > 200 lines, modified public API, touched security-relevant paths) or standard review (small refactor, dependency bump, style changes). Deep PRs go to the reasoning model with a 10 000-token thinking budget; standard PRs go to a haiku-class model at 10× lower cost.

import anthropic
import subprocess

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def classify_pr(diff: str, files_changed: list[str]) -> str:
    """Heuristic classifier — no model call needed."""
    SECURITY_PATHS = {"auth/", "middleware/", "permissions/", "secrets/"}
    API_PATTERNS   = {"def ", "class ", "export "}

    lines_changed = diff.count("\n+") + diff.count("\n-")
    touches_security = any(p in f for f in files_changed for p in SECURITY_PATHS)
    changes_api = any(p in diff for p in API_PATTERNS)

    if lines_changed > 200 or touches_security or changes_api:
        return "deep"
    return "standard"

def deep_review(diff: str, context: str) -> str:
    """Extended thinking review for complex PRs."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=14000,
        thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000},
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""You are a senior engineer.
Review this pull request thoroughly:
- Correctness and edge-case bugs
- Security vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10)
- API breaking changes
- Missing test coverage
- Performance regressions

Repository context: {context}

Diff:
{diff}

Output severity-tagged findings (CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW) with line references."""}],
    )
    for block in resp.content:
        if block.type == "text":
            return block.text
    return ""

def standard_review(diff: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=1500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Quick code review:\n{diff}"}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# In a CI pipeline hook:
diff = subprocess.check_output(["git", "diff", "main...HEAD"]).decode(errors="replace")
files = subprocess.check_output(["git", "diff", "--name-only", "main...HEAD"]).decode().splitlines()

tier = classify_pr(diff, files)
if tier == "deep":
    review = deep_review(diff, "Python FastAPI, PostgreSQL, 80k DAU, PCI-DSS scope")
    print("[DEEP REVIEW]")
else:
    review = standard_review(diff)
    print("[STANDARD REVIEW]")

print(review[:2000])

This routing pattern typically routes 65–75% of PRs to the standard tier with no measurable quality loss, while the deep tier provides thorough analysis—including security and API-contract checks—on the subset of PRs where it actually matters.
Using reasoning models on every agent hop

Sending every subtask—including simple tool-result formatting and retrieval queries—to a reasoning model inflates cost by 10–50× relative to a tiered design. The fix is explicit routing: classify task complexity before model selection, not after.
Setting max_tokens too close to budget_tokens
parameter must cover both thinking tokens and output tokens. Setting
leaves zero tokens for the visible answer, causing the API to return an empty text block or a
stop reason without output. Always set
Exposing raw thinking traces to end users

Thinking traces include exploratory and sometimes erroneous intermediate steps that were corrected before the final answer. Surfacing these verbatim to users degrades trust ("why did it say that?"), violates most providers' terms of service, and may leak internal reasoning that contradicts the final answer. Always filter on
Assuming reasoning eliminates the need for tool verification

A reasoning model produces structurally sounder tool calls than a standard model, but it does not produce correct tool calls 100% of the time. External state—file system, database schema, API contracts—can contradict what the model's internal deliberation assumed. Always run the output through a deterministic check before executing destructive operations.
Passing thinking blocks across conversation turns naively

Some APIs require that thinking blocks returned in an assistant turn be included verbatim when building the next user turn (the API rejects truncated or modified thinking content). Failing to preserve the exact thinking block in multi-turn conversations causes API errors or silent context corruption.
Implement a task-complexity classifier (heuristic or small model) that gates access to the reasoning tier. Default to the cheap model and escalate only on failure or measured complexity.

Sample 50–100 representative tasks, run them at 2k, 4k, 8k, and 16k budget tokens, and plot accuracy vs. cost. Many tasks hit their ceiling at 4k–6k tokens; beyond that, quality gains flatten while cost continues to rise.
Strip thinking before forwarding to users.
Maintain two response objects: the developer record (thinking + answer) for observability, and the user record (answer only) for display.
Pair reasoning model outputs with executable checks: run generated code in a sandbox, validate JSON against a schema, or assert plan preconditions before committing to side effects.
Cache plans when inputs are stable.

If the same high-level planning problem appears across many similar requests (same task schema, same tool set), cache the reasoning model's structured plan and re-parameterise it for each request rather than re-invoking the expensive model.
Alert on requests where thinking tokens exceed 80% of budget—these may indicate underspecified tasks or prompts that cause the model to thrash rather than converge.

Reasoning model latency scales roughly linearly with thinking budget. Empirical measurements across several providers show approximately 0.5–1.5ms per thinking token on hosted endpoints, meaning a 10 000-token budget adds 5–15 seconds of wall-clock latency before the first output token appears. For interactive applications this is prohibitive; for background or batch workloads it is acceptable.
Because thinking tokens are priced identically to output tokens, the effective cost per useful output token is multiplied by the ratio

. A request with 8 000 thinking tokens and 500 output tokens costs 17× more than the same 500-token output without thinking. Cost-optimised deployments minimise this ratio by routing only tasks that genuinely require deep deliberation.

Providers that support prompt caching (e.g., Anthropic's cache-control headers) allow the system prompt and static context to be cached across requests. For reasoning models this is especially valuable because the context window is large and the fixed prefix (tool schemas, background knowledge, domain instructions) is expensive to re-process. Enabling caching on a 5 000-token system prompt can reduce per-request input cost by 60–80% at sufficient request volume.

Separate model IDs from the GPT-4 family.

Available on HuggingFace and self-hostable via vLLM. The model produces
blocks that applications parse from the completion text.
Provides native support for routing nodes to different model tiers based on state. Define a
node that branches between a reasoning-model planner node and a standard executor node.
Supports multi-model agent runners where individual reasoning steps can be assigned to different LLM configurations.
discriminated unions making it straightforward to filter and process blocks separately.

Fine-tuned LLMs and reasoning models are complementary: fine-tuning specialises the model on domain format and vocabulary (reducing context overhead), while the reasoning training ensures robust multi-step deliberation. For production systems, a fine-tuned reasoning model (e.g., distilled reasoning checkpoints fine-tuned on domain data) often outperforms either technique alone.

Thinking tokens process the same input as the visible response. If adversarial content (e.g., a document the model is asked to summarise) contains instruction-injection payloads, the thinking scratchpad may follow those instructions and produce a manipulated final answer. The countermeasure is strict input sanitisation and, where possible, isolating untrusted content in structured tool-result blocks rather than injecting it directly into the user turn.

Thinking traces may contain information the model inferred from the context—confidential documents, PII, secrets embedded in tool results—even if that information was not requested in the final answer. Logging all thinking traces to observability systems requires the same access controls as the underlying data. Thinking traces must never be forwarded to users.

A reasoning model can "think" that its answer is correct while still being wrong. The scratchpad produces a coherent narrative justifying the answer, which can look convincing even when the conclusion is flawed. For high-stakes actions (irreversible API calls, database mutations, financial transactions), always gate on a deterministic external check rather than the model's self-assessment.

Adversarial prompts can be crafted to force the reasoning model to consume its full thinking budget without converging on an answer, maximising per-request cost. Rate-limit reasoning-model endpoints more aggressively than standard endpoints, and set per-user and per-session spending caps in addition to per-request

When the context window fills (long conversation history plus large thinking blocks), the model may truncate thinking prematurely and emit a lower-quality answer without warning. Monitor token usage per request; when approaching 80% of the context window, summarise or compact the conversation history before the next reasoning call.

Think of a standard LLM as a contractor who gives an instant verbal estimate on a job: fast, useful for simple tasks, but prone to mistakes on complex ones because there is no time to think it through. A reasoning model is the same contractor who, before speaking, sits down with a notepad, sketches out the problem, crosses off wrong approaches, double-checks the plan, and
gives the estimate. The notepad (thinking tokens) costs time and paper (latency and tokens); the spoken estimate (visible output) is more reliable because the analysis happened before commitment.
The key intuition for agents is the

: the notepad-using contractor designs the plan once; a team of faster workers (executor models) carries it out step by step. This pattern minimises expensive deliberation while preserving its quality benefit at the decision points that matter most.

Reasoning models are trained with RL on verifiable tasks, which teaches them to generate extended internal deliberation (thinking tokens) before producing a final answer—quality comes from the training signal, not a symbolic solver.

In agentic systems, reasoning models fill the planner and critic roles; cheaper executor models handle the high-frequency, low-complexity steps. This split drives cost down while preserving quality where it matters most.

The core trade-off is accuracy vs. latency and cost: thinking tokens are billed like output tokens, and a 10 000-token thinking budget adds 5–15 seconds of latency. Route selectively and calibrate budgets empirically.

Thinking traces must never be forwarded to end users: they may contain exploratory wrong paths, intermediate contradictions, or sensitive data inferred from context, and exposing them typically violates provider policies.

Deterministic verification (unit tests, schema validation, sandbox execution) remains necessary after reasoning model outputs—the scratchpad produces a convincing justification for the answer, but cannot guarantee external correctness.
Prompt caching on the system prompt and large static context can reduce per-request cost by 60–80% at volume, making reasoning model deployments substantially more economical in production pipelines.

Distilled reasoning models transfer the teacher model's chain-of-thought patterns into smaller, faster checkpoints, making reasoning capability available at lower latency for use cases where the full model's budget is excessive.

Adversarial prompts can exploit thinking chains via injection and can exhaust thinking budgets to maximise cost; apply the same input sanitisation and rate-limiting to reasoning endpoints as to any high-value API surface.
